title: What US assets are held overseas?
gist: As of April 2026, foreign investors' US financial asset portfolio is 59.4% equities and only ~23.7% Treasuries combined, a sharp reversal from the crisis-era flight to safety when Treasuries peaked at 36.5% of foreign holdings in 2009.

insights.claim: As of April 2026, US equities (common stock, preferred stock, and fund shares) made up 59.4% of the overall portfolio of US financial assets held overseas, the single largest class.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Foreign demand for US assets is dominated by equities, so global risk appetite (not just rates) drives capital flows into US markets.
insights.quote: made up 59.4% of the overall portfolio of US financial assets held overseas

insights.claim: As of April 2026, long-term Treasury securities (maturing more than a year out) were 19.9% and short-term Treasury bills 3.8% of US financial assets held overseas, so all Treasuries combined are roughly 23.7% versus equities' 59.4%.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Treasuries are a minority of foreign US holdings, meaning headlines about foreign Treasury demand overstate their weight in the broader foreign portfolio.
insights.quote: Bonds issued by the US Treasury and maturing more than a year into the future amount to 19.9%

insights.claim: Long-term corporate bonds (a category that also includes state, local, and municipal bonds) represented 13.3% of US financial assets held overseas as of April 2026.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Foreign credit exposure to US corporates and munis is non-trivial, a channel worth watching for spread and demand shifts.

insights.claim: Long-term agency bonds issued by Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae were just 3.6% of US financial assets held overseas as of April 2026, the smallest of the listed classes.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Foreign ownership of US mortgage-agency debt is a small slice, limiting one potential source of forced-selling risk in that market.
insights.quote: Those bonds are issued by US federal agencies Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, and Ginnie Mae, representing 3.6%

themes.theme: Historical shift in foreign portfolio mix

insights.claim: Between 1984 and 2008, equities made up roughly one third of foreign portfolios of US assets, a much smaller share than the 59.4% seen in April 2026.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: The rising equity share reflects a multi-decade structural tilt in how the world allocates to US assets.
insights.quote: Between 1984 and 2008, equities made up roughly one third of foreign portfolios of US assets
insights.locator: The graph also shows a shifting mix

insights.claim: The 2007-2009 Great Recession triggered a flight to safety in which foreign holdings of risk-free Treasury securities peaked at 36.5% of US assets held overseas in 2009.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Crisis episodes reliably shift foreign allocation toward Treasuries, a signal to watch for repeat behavior in future risk-off events.
insights.quote: the 2007-2009 Great Recession greatly boosted holdings of risk-free Treasury securities, and those peaked at 36.5% in 2009

insights.claim: Since the 2009 Treasury peak, equities have gradually reclaimed their dominant share of foreign portfolios of US assets, now amounting to more than half of total value.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: The reversal back into equities implies foreign investors have re-embraced US risk assets, supporting valuations but raising exposure if sentiment turns.
insights.quote: equities gradually reclaimed their lion’s share of foreign portfolios of US assets, amounting to more than half of their overall value
insights.locator: Between then and the time of this writing
